request.question: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?

title: Eli Jaxon-Bear
Hervey Alan
Jan van der Watt
Peter Bos
Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)
Der Bärenhäuter (opera)
Christopher Larkin (conductor)
Rick van der Linden
Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland
Derek Hammond-Stroud
sentences: Eli Jaxon-Bear (born Elliot J. Zeldow in 1947) is an American spiritual teacher and author.
He lives in Ashland, Oregon with his wife, fellow spiritual teacher Gangaji.
Before he met his teacher, Sri H.W.L. Poonja, in 1990, he was best known for his work on the spiritual dimension of the Enneagram.
Following his meeting with Poonjaji he continued to teach, carrying his teacher’s message to the West.
Hervey Alan (22 February 1910 – 12 January 1982) was an English operatic bass and voice teacher.

During his career he sang leading roles with most of Great Britain's major opera institutions, including the Edinburgh Festival, the Glyndebourne Festival, the Royal Opera House, the Sadler's Wells Opera, and the Welsh National Opera.
He is best known for creating the role of Mr. Redburn in the world premiere of Benjamin Britten's "Billy Budd" at the Royal Opera House, London, on 1 December 1951.
Music critic Elizabeth Forbes wrote that his voice was "dark toned, resonant", and "especially effective as Zaccaria in "Nabucco".

Jan Gabriël Van der Watt (born 5 November 1952) is a South African biblical scholar and Bible translator who moved to the Netherlands in 2009 to take up a chair in New Testament and Source texts of early Christianity at Radboud University in Nijmegen.

For a quarter of a century previously, he was professor at the University of Pretoria, where he was named as one of the 100 most influential academic thinkers in the 100-year history of the University of Pretoria, South Africa (see www.up.ac.za under Leading Minds).

Van der Watt was also rated as international acknowledged researcher that is regarded by some of his international peers as international leader in his field, (see www.nrf.ac.za under rated researchers).
Van der Watt is internationally best known for his monograph: Family of the King: Dynamics of Metaphor in the Gosepl According to John.

Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.
He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.
Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".
Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.
As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Christopher Larkin is an American conductor who is best known for his work within the field of opera.
He was Associate Conductor of the Houston Grand Opera for a number of years where he notably conducted the world premieres of Mark Adamo's "Little Women" and Michael Daugherty’s "Jackie O".
He is also the former Music Director of the New York City Opera National Company.

As a guest conductor, Larkin has worked for numerous opera houses including Wexford Festival Opera, Santa Fe Opera, Washington National Opera, Portland Opera, Florentine Opera, Opera Pacific, Opera Carolina, and Boston Lyric Opera among many others.
He has also worked as an Assistant Conductor and vocal coach for the Metropolitan Opera, San Francisco Opera, Opera Company of Philadelphia, Vancouver Opera, and the Canadian Opera Company.
Rick van der Linden (5 August 1946, Badhoevedorp, North Holland - 22 January 2006, Groningen) was a Dutch composer and keyboardist.
Van der Linden first gained fame as a member of Ekseption, but he played in several other bands including most notably Trace, as well as solo.
Van der Linden was best known for his reworkings of classical music in a pop music domain, often with jazz improvisations.

"Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland " (original: "Nu kom der Heyden heyland ", English: "Now come, Saviour of the heathens") is a Lutheran chorale of 1524 with words written by Martin Luther, based on "Veni redemptor gentium " by Ambrose.
It was printed in the Erfurt "Enchiridion" of 1524.
The chorale was used as the prominent hymn for the first Sunday of Advent for centuries.

It was used widely in organ settings by Protestant baroque composers, most notably Johann Sebastian Bach: he set it as the opening chorale prelude BWV 599 of Orgelbüchlein; and three times—as BWV 659 (one of his best known organ compositions), BWV 660 and BWV 661—in his Great Eighteen Chorale Preludes.

Bach used the hymn in his chorale cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 62 (1724) and in the opening chorale fantasia of his earlier cantata "Nun komm, der Heiden Heiland", BWV 61 , BWV 61 (1714).
Max Reger composed a chorale prelude as No. 29 of his 52 Chorale Preludes, Op. 67 in 1902.

Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".
He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: For which opera was the teacher of Der Bärenhäuter best known?
hop1_docs: Der Bärenhäuter (opera) | Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.  As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
Der Bärenhäuter | Der Bärenhäuter (German "Bearskin man") may refer to:
Bearskin (film) | Der Bärenhäuter is a German movie from the year 1986, based on the fairy tale "Bearskin".

Peter Bos | Petrus Andreas Maria (Peter) Bos (born 20 June 1950 in Amsterdam) is a Dutch film, stage and television actor best known for his role in the 1982 children's television series "De Zevensprong" in which he played the leading part as the teacher Frans van der Steg.  He is also known from the children's film "Knokken voor twee" (directed by Karst van der Meulen) which was released in the same year.

Der Prozeß (opera) | Der Prozeß ("English: The Trial ") is a German-language opera in two parts, divided into nine scenes, with music by Gottfried von Einem and a libretto by Boris Blacher and , based on the novel by Franz Kafka.  Composed over the period 1950 to 1952, this was von Einem's second opera.  He dedicated it to the psychologist and theologian Oskar Pfister, who had been his therapist, and to his former teacher, Karl Christian Jensen.

Derek Hammond-Stroud | Derek Hammond-Stroud, OBE (10 January 192614 May 2012) was an English baritone opera singer best known for his performances of German lieder and his international performances in opera, particularly the roles of Alberich in Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen", Herr Faninal in "Der Rosenkavalier" and Beckmesser in "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg".  He also made recordings, including a series of recordings of the Gilbert and Sullivan patter roles.

Pomona (opera) | Der Sieg der fruchtbaren Pomona (The Victory of Fruitful Pomona) is a German-language opera by Reinhard Keiser to a libretto by Christian Heinrich Postel which premiered at the Hamburg Opera on 19 October 1702, for the birthday of Frederick IV of Denmark.  The plot was based on the legend of Pomona and Jupiter, and the libretto was the last of a series which the elderly poet Postel had provided to the young Keiser during the five previous opera seasons.  The opera was the first of four Keiser operas dedicated to the Danish crown.
response.summary_1:
Facts from passages:
- [1] "1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner" — Der Bärenhäuter is by Siegfried Wagner. 
- [1] "more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck" — the opera is said to be influenced by Siegfried Wagner's teacher, named Humperdinck.
Missing / ambiguous bridge entity:
- Bridge entity = "Humperdinck" (teacher). The passages do not give a full name or identify which Humperdinck is meant. This is ambiguous between at least: Engelbert Humperdinck (composer) and Engelbert Humperdinck (singer) — no Humperdinck page is present in the supplied passages.
Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity and find the teacher's best-known opera:
1) Fetch the page "Engelbert Humperdinck (composer)" — extract the lead sentence, infobox 'Notable works' or 'Known for', and any line saying "best known for" or listing his most famous opera (check for the opera title, e.g. the "Hänsel und Gretel" entry) .
2) Fetch the page "Siegfried Wagner" — extract the biography/education/teachers section or infobox 'Teacher' to confirm the teacher's full name as cited for Der Bärenhäuter.
3) If needed, fetch "Humperdinck (disambiguation)" — extract listed persons and links to composer vs. singer pages to confirm which Humperdinck is relevant.
Do not answer yet: the supplied passages lack the teacher's full identity and any statement of which opera that teacher is best known for; retrieve the targets above to answer.
response.hop2_query: Humperdinck Siegfried Wagner teacher best-known opera
hop2_docs: Der Bärenhäuter (opera) | Der Bärenhäuter is an 1899 German-language opera by Siegfried Wagner based on the German folk tale Der Bärenhäuter, or the Bearskin man.  As with most of Siegfried Wagner's operas it is more influenced by his teacher Humperdinck than his father Richard Wagner's operas.
List of operas by Siegfried Wagner | This is a complete list of the operas of the German composer Siegfried Wagner (1869–1930), the son of Richard Wagner (1813–1883).

Siegfried Wagner | Siegfried Wagner (6 June 18694 August 1930) was a German composer and conductor, the son of Richard Wagner.  He was an opera composer and the artistic director of the Bayreuth Festival from 1908 to 1930.
Engelbert Humperdinck (composer) | Engelbert Humperdinck (] ; 1 September 1854 – 27 September 1921) was a German composer, best known for his opera "Hansel and Gretel".

Wolfgang Wagner | Wolfgang Wagner (30 August 191921 March 2010) was a German opera director.  He is best known as the director (Festspielleiter) of the Bayreuth Festival, a position he initially assumed alongside his brother Wieland in 1951 until the latter's death in 1966.  From then on, he assumed total control until he retired in 2008, although many of the productions which he commissioned were severely criticized in their day.  He had been plagued by family conflicts and criticism for many years.  He was the son of Siegfried Wagner, the grandson of Richard Wagner, and the great-grandson of Franz Liszt.

Carmen Reppel | Carmen Reppel is a German soprano active in both opera and concert.  A member of the Staatstheater Hannover , she had a major international career, appearing in leading roles in over 60 operas.  She also explored lesser-known repertoire such as Siegfried Wagner's "Schwarzschwanenreich " and appeared in contemporary operas, singing in world premieres of operas by Aribert Reimann and Flavio Testi.  She is known for her parts in the "Jahrhundertring ", the centenary production of Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" at the Bayreuth Festival, including the filmed version of 1980.

Jay Hunter Morris | Jay Hunter Morris (born July 3, 1963) is an American operatic tenor.  He is best known internationally for the role of Siegfried in the Metropolitan Opera's 2011–12 series of Wagner's "Ring Cycle", performances of which were cinecast and radio broadcast live worldwide, aired on U.S. television, and released on DVD.
hop2_titles: List of operas by Siegfried Wagner
response.summary_2: Humperdinck (the teacher referenced for Siegfried Wagner's Der Bärenhäuter) was Engelbert Humperdinck, best known for the opera Hansel and Gretel. Engelbert Humperdinck was a German composer (1854–1921).
